title: Chapter 9: The Forbidden Margin
content: The text was hidden in the back of *Loopholes in the Ancient Laws*, written in a hand so small and cramped that Sera had missed it on her first three readings. It was crammed into the margin of a page discussing the Blood Curse, squeezed between paragraphs like a secret someone had been afraid to say out loud.

*The Dominant Pact is forbidden by all laws of heaven and earth. It turns the anchor into a slave, the vampire into a tyrant. It has not been attempted in six thousand years. But if it were — if some desperate fool tried to rewrite the bond — the connection would flow one way. The vampire would not share power. The vampire would TAKE power. From the anchor. From the target. From anyone unlucky enough to be touching the bond when it inverted.*
*The price, of course, is the anchor's soul. But in extremis, what is a soul compared to victory?*

Sera read the passage aloud in the salt mine cache, her voice steady despite the content. Kael sat across from her, a wooden stake in one hand and a whetstone in the other, sharpening the point in slow, methodical strokes.

"That's it," she said. "That's what I was looking for. The reason the Tribunal suppressed Blood Pact research for three thousand years. It's not the daylight immunity they're afraid of — it's the Dominant Pact. The possibility that a vampire could use the bond as a weapon instead of a shield."
"Against other vampires," Kael said.

"Against anyone. The anchor, an attacking vampire, even a Prince who tries to sever the bond." She tapped the margin text. "It says 'from the target.' That means the Dominant Pact isn't just about controlling the anchor. It's about reaching through the bond and stealing power from whoever is threatening it. A defensive mechanism, weaponized."
Kael set down the stake. "If I used it against Dredd..."

"You'd drain him. Not just his heart-blood — his *everything*. His rank, his territory, his accumulated strength. Two hundred years of power, flowing into you through the Pact like water through a broken dam." She looked up at him. "But you'd also drain me. The anchor always pays the price. My humanity, what's left of it, would be the fuel."
"I'm not using it against Dredd. The ceiling collapse is enough. Sunlight will kill him."
"Then why are you learning about it?"

"Because Dredd isn't the last enemy. Orsi is out there. The Tribunal is out there. Prince Marius is out there, sitting on a throne of bones for ten thousand years, and someday he's going to notice that a fledgling who should have died in the first week has become something he can't control." Kael picked up the stake and resumed sharpening. "When that day comes, I need every weapon I can find. Even the forbidden ones."
Sera was quiet for a long moment. The candle flame danced. Outside, the salt tunnels hummed their endless, crystalline song.
"Promise me something else," she said.
"What?"
"If you ever use it — the Dominant Pact — don't use it on me. Use it on them. The enemies. The Princes. The ones who deserve to lose everything."
Kael stopped sharpening. "Sera—"

"I know what I am. I know what the Pact is doing to me. Every day, I'm a little less human and a little more yours. The transformation isn't stopping. It's accelerating." She held up her hand, and the golden veins pulsed beneath her skin. "But I'm still me. I'm still the paladin who chose to trust a fledgling in a ruined church. If the time comes when you have to burn my soul to save yourself... promise me it will be for something that matters. Not survival. *Victory*."
The whetstone slipped. Kael caught it before it hit the floor.
"I promise," he said.

He didn't know if he meant it. He didn't know if promises like that could be kept. But he said it anyway, because Sera needed to hear it, and because somewhere in the cold machinery of his new vampire heart, there was still a part of him that was human. Still a part of him that was Kael Ashworth, the scout who'd volunteered for dawn patrols because he loved the sun.
The next morning, Kael made his final reconnaissance of the Keep.

He walked through Greyhaven at noon, alone, the streets empty and silent. Every vampire in the city was in torpor — shopkeepers slumped over their counters, Barons curled in shadowed alcoves, thralls frozen mid-task with trays of congealed blood. He could have killed any of them. It would have been easy. Just stakes through the heart, one by one, and Dredd's entire city would be ash before sunset.
But killing them would trigger the Blood Curse. And he had bigger plans for the Curse.

He stopped at the outer wall. The bone and iron gate loomed above him, unguarded, unwarded, open. Beyond it, the Keep's spiraling corridors stretched upward into darkness. The throne room waited at the top, where a two-hundred-year-old Viscount dreamed of blood and tribute and the slow decay of his rotten kingdom.
*Twelve hits*, Marcus had said. *Twelve hits to the right support beam, and the ceiling comes down.*

Kael wound up and punched the wall beside the gate. Not hard enough to break it — just hard enough to feel the stone's resistance, to calculate the force he'd need. Baron strength was different from human strength. It flowed through muscles that had been rebuilt by vampiric transformation, through bones that had shattered and reformed during the heart-blood consumption. He was stronger than he'd ever been. Strong enough to crack ancient stone. Strong enough to bring a ceiling down on a sleeping tyrant.
*Tomorrow*, he thought. *Tomorrow at noon, I end the Rot King.*
He turned and walked back through the empty streets of Greyhaven, a single vampire moving through a city of the dead, and no one was awake to see him go.
